headline: New Hope for Alzheimer's Sleep: Immune Cells, Not Plaques, Are the Culprit
meta_description: University of Kentucky researchers discover brain immune cells cause Alzheimer's sleep loss, restoring two hours of daily sleep without removing plaques.
summary_short: Scientists at the University of Kentucky identified why Alzheimer's patients lose sleep—and it's not the plaques themselves. The answer opens a new treatment path.
body:
Sleep loss is one of Alzheimer's cruelest symptoms, stealing rest night after night. For years, researchers assumed sticky protein plaques or damaged neurons were to blame. But scientists at the University of Kentucky just upended that understanding.

Microglia—the brain's own immune cells—are the real troublemakers. When these cells react to amyloid plaques, they trigger a cascade of inflammation so intense it keeps the entire brain awake, as if "the microglia are partying all night," explained researcher Shannon L. Macauley.

In a study published in *Alzheimer's & Dementia*, the team tested this theory using mice genetically prone to developing plaques. They used a drug called Pexidartinib to temporarily remove about 87% of these immune cells. The result was striking: the mice regained more than two hours of sleep per day—despite the plaques remaining unchanged.

What makes this finding truly surprising is the ceiling effect the researchers discovered. When plaque burden more than doubled between six and 18 months of age, the sleep disruption didn't worsen proportionally. This suggests that early immune activation alone may establish the sleep problem.

For people living with Alzheimer's, this opens a paradigm-shifting possibility: treating sleep loss might not require clearing plaques at all—just calming the brain's overactive immune response.
